title: Body dysmorphic disorder
vignette: A 27-year-old woman comes to the office due to concerns about her weight. She is frustrated about gaining a few pounds, although she maintains a well-balanced diet and exercises for an hour 3 times a week. The patient has been worried for years that her thighs are "huge" and "covered in cellulite." She always wears loose-fitting pants and spends hours a day researching leg exercises and measuring the circumference of her thighs. The patient generally stays at home and avoids social activities as she feels people tend to stare at her. She weighs 58 kg (127.9 lb) and is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall. Physical examination is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

clues.text: her thighs are "huge" and "covered in cellulite
clues.note: A preoccupation with a perceived physical defect (thighs) that is not observable to others is the core of body dysmorphic disorder. Her body is normal, yet she's convinced of a flaw.

clues.text: spends hours a day researching leg exercises and measuring the circumference of her thighs
clues.note: Repetitive behaviors (checking, measuring, researching) performed in response to the appearance preoccupation are characteristic of body dysmorphic disorder. The hours-a-day rituals cause impairment.

clues.text: avoids social activities as she feels people tend to stare
clues.note: Avoidance and significant social impairment driven by the appearance concern support body dysmorphic disorder (not social anxiety per se, which is fear of scrutiny/embarrassment generally).

whatMatters: Years-long preoccupation with a perceived bodily defect (thighs) that others don't see, with time-consuming checking/measuring rituals and social avoidance, in a normal-weight woman = body dysmorphic disorder. Normal weight and focus on a body PART (not overall weight/fear of fatness) distinguish it from anorexia.
strategy: For appearance-focused distress, separate BDD from eating disorders: BDD = fixation on a specific perceived defect with checking rituals, normal weight; anorexia/bulimia = distorted body image centered on WEIGHT with restriction/binge-purge and (for anorexia) low weight. Normal BMI + body-part obsession = BDD.

eliminations.label: agoraphobia — fear of escape, not appearance
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: agoraphobia is fear/avoidance of situations where escape is hard or help unavailable (crowds, transit, open spaces). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: her avoidance is driven by an appearance concern, not fear of being trapped/unable to escape. ✅ Correct if: avoidance were motivated by panic/escape concerns in open or enclosed public spaces.

eliminations.label: anorexia nervosa — needs low weight/weight focus
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: anorexia nervosa features restriction, intense fear of weight gain, and low body weight with distorted body image. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: she is NORMAL weight and fixates on a specific body PART (thighs/cellulite), not overall weight/fear of fatness with restriction. ✅ Correct if: she were underweight with restriction and fear of gaining weight.

eliminations.label: avoidant personality disorder — pervasive inadequacy, not a defect fixation
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: avoidant PD is a pervasive pattern of social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, and hypersensitivity to criticism. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: her impairment centers on a specific appearance preoccupation with rituals, not a lifelong personality pattern of feeling inadequate. ✅ Correct if: there were a long-standing pattern of avoiding relationships due to fear of rejection/inadequacy.

eliminations.label: bulimia nervosa — needs binge/purge
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: bulimia nervosa involves binge eating with compensatory behaviors (vomiting, laxatives, excessive exercise) and weight-focused self-evaluation, usually normal weight. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: there's no binge-purge behavior; her concern is a perceived body-part defect with checking rituals. ✅ Correct if: there were recurrent binges with compensatory purging.

eliminations.label: delusional disorder — fixed false belief, but appearance-based here
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: delusional disorder is a fixed false belief without other psychotic features. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: BDD beliefs can range to delusional, but the disorder is specifically about a perceived APPEARANCE defect with compulsive behaviors — classified as BDD (with or without insight), not a generic delusional disorder. ✅ Correct if: the fixed false belief were nonappearance-related (e.g., persecutory).

eliminations.label: OCD — obsessions/compulsions but not appearance-specific
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: OCD features intrusive obsessions and compulsions (often contamination, checking, symmetry) that are NOT focused on appearance. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: her obsessions/compulsions are specifically about a perceived bodily defect, which defines BDD (an OCD-related disorder) rather than OCD. ✅ Correct if: the obsessions/compulsions were nonappearance-related (contamination, harm, symmetry).

eliminations.label: social anxiety disorder — fear of scrutiny generally
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: social anxiety disorder is marked fear of social/performance situations due to possible embarrassment. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: her social avoidance stems from a specific appearance preoccupation (thighs), not a general fear of negative evaluation. ✅ Correct if: she feared embarrassment in social/performance settings broadly, without a body-defect fixation.

reasoning: Preoccupation with a perceived defect (thighs) not observable to others
Time-consuming repetitive behaviors (measuring/researching) and social avoidance
Normal weight, focus on a body PART rather than overall weight/fear of fatness
mechanism: Body dysmorphic disorder is an obsessive-compulsive-related disorder defined by preoccupation with one or more perceived physical defects that are not observable or appear slight to others, accompanied by repetitive behaviors (mirror checking, grooming, measuring, reassurance seeking) or mental acts in response. It causes significant distress/impairment, often leading to avoidance and unnecessary cosmetic procedures; insight ranges from good to absent/delusional. First-line treatment is SSRIs and CBT.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Psych > OCD and related disorders: body dysmorphic disorder (perceived defect, repetitive behaviors, impairment) vs eating disorders, social anxiety, OCD, avoidant PD. Treatment: SSRIs + CBT.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Pin the focus: BDD = a specific perceived APPEARANCE defect with rituals at NORMAL weight. Contrast with anorexia (weight/low BMI) and social anxiety (general scrutiny fear).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on body dysmorphic disorder, preoccupation vs delusion, compulsion, distorted body image, OCD-related disorders? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Anorexia and social anxiety are the main traps — but she's normal weight (not anorexia) and avoids people because of a SPECIFIC body defect (not general scrutiny). That's BDD.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: High national correct rate (91%) — if you missed it, just nail the BDD vs anorexia distinction (body-part defect at normal weight vs weight-focused low BMI).

keywords.front: Preoccupation with a perceived defect + checking rituals (normal weight)

keywords.back: Mirror checking, measuring, grooming, reassurance seeking
BDD = body-part defect at normal weight; anorexia = weight focus + low BMI
Fear of scrutiny/embarrassment in social situations (general)
Fear of situations where escape is difficult

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry > OCD-related disorders: body dysmorphic disorder — criteria, repetitive behaviors, insight spectrum, SSRI/CBT treatment.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry > OCD-related disorders: body dysmorphic disorder — criteria, repetitive behaviors, insight spectrum, SSRI/CBT treatment.

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry > anorexia/bulimia (weight-focused), social anxiety disorder, agoraphobia, avoidant PD, OCD, delusional disorder — distinguishing features.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry > anorexia/bulimia (weight-focused), social anxiety disorder, agoraphobia, avoidant PD, OCD, delusional disorder — distinguishing features.

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry > obsessive-compulsive and related disorders (OCD, BDD, hoarding, trichotillomania) and their shared treatment.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry > obsessive-compulsive and related disorders (OCD, BDD, hoarding, trichotillomania) and their shared treatment.

memoryHook: Normal body, hours spent measuring an imagined 'huge' thigh = Body Dysmorphic Disorder (defect fixation + rituals), not anorexia (which needs low weight).
